title: Purpose & Condition — Aby, Kdyby
description: Learn two powerful Czech conjunctions for complex sentences: aby (so that / in order to — expressing purpose) and kdyby (if — expressing hypothetical or contrary-to-fact conditions). Practice the unique grammar patterns where both conjunctions require past-tense verb forms and gender agreement. Build rich conditional sentences combining both conjunctions.

options: a purpose (so that / in order to)
data.explanation: 'Aby' = so that / in order to — expresses purpose or intention. Example: 'Učím se česky, abych rozuměl lidem.' = I'm learning Czech so that I can understand people. The verb after 'aby' is always in the past-tense form (the -l participle) with gender agreement.

exercises.answer_key: a purpose (so that / in order to)

exercises.prompt: What is the correct 'aby' form for 'já' (I, masculine speaker)?

data.question_cz: Jaký je správný tvar 'aby' pro 'já' (mužský mluvčí)?
data.question_en: What is the correct 'aby' form for 'I' (masculine speaker)?
data.explanation: 'Aby' conjugates like a past-tense conditional: abych (I), abys (you sg.), aby (he/she/it), abychom (we), abyste (you pl.), aby (they). The verb following aby is in the past participle (-l form): 'abych dělal' (masc.), 'abych dělala' (fem.).

exercises.prompt: Complete with the correct 'aby' form: 'I'm learning Czech so that I can speak with friends.' (male speaker)

data.sentence: Učím se česky, ___ mohl mluvit s kamarády.
data.explanation: 'Učím se česky, abych mohl mluvit s kamarády.' = I'm learning Czech so that I can speak with friends. 'Abych' is the 1st person singular form. The verb after 'abych' is 'mohl' (past participle of 'moci/to be able', masc.) followed by the infinitive 'mluvit'. Aby + past participle + (optionally) infinitive.

exercises.prompt: Complete with the correct 'aby' form + past participle: 'I told him so that he would know.'

data.sentence: Řekl jsem mu to, ___ to ___. (on — vědět)
data.explanation: 'Řekl jsem mu to, aby to věděl.' = I told him so that he would know. 'Aby' = 3rd person singular (he). 'Věděl' = past participle of 'vědět' (masculine, agreeing with 'on'). The aby-clause uses past-tense forms regardless of when the action happens — this is a grammatical requirement, not a time reference.

exercises.prompt: What does 'kdyby' mean and when is it used?

data.question_cz: Co znamená 'kdyby' a kdy se používá?
data.question_en: What does 'kdyby' mean and when is it used?
options: if — for hypothetical or contrary-to-fact situations
data.explanation: 'Kdyby' = if (hypothetical / contrary-to-fact). It expresses unreal conditions. Example: 'Kdybych měl čas, šel bych s tebou.' = If I had time, I would go with you (but I don't have time). The result clause uses the conditional 'bych/bys/by...'. This is different from 'jestli' (if/whether — expressing factual uncertainty).

exercises.answer_key: if — for hypothetical or contrary-to-fact situations

exercises.prompt: Complete with the correct 'kdyby' form: 'If I had more money, I would buy a new car.' (male speaker)

data.sentence: ___ měl víc peněz, koupil bych si nové auto.
data.explanation: 'Kdybych měl víc peněz, koupil bych si nové auto.' = If I had more money, I would buy a new car. Kdyby conjugation: kdybych (I), kdybys (you sg.), kdyby (he/she/it), kdybychom (we), kdybyste (you pl.), kdyby (they). The result clause uses conditional 'koupil bych' (I would buy). Both verbs use past participle + gender agreement.

exercises.prompt: Complete with 'kdyby' form + past participle: 'If he knew the truth, he would be angry.'

data.explanation: 'Kdyby věděl pravdu, byl by naštvaný.' = If he knew the truth, he would be angry. Structure: kdyby + past participle (věděl) → main clause: past participle (byl) + conditional particle (by). Both parts use the -l participle! This is a classic contrary-to-fact conditional.

exercises.prompt: How does the verb form after 'kdyby' compare to the verb form after 'aby'?

data.question_cz: Jaký je rozdíl ve tvaru slovesa po 'kdyby' a po 'aby'?
data.question_en: How does the verb form after 'kdyby' compare to the verb form after 'aby'?
options: Both use the past participle (-l form)
Kdyby uses present tense, aby uses future
Kdyby uses infinitive, aby uses past participle
data.explanation: Both 'kdyby' and 'aby' require the past participle (-l form) of the verb with gender agreement! Examples: 'abych udělal' (so that I do — masc.), 'kdybych udělal' (if I did — masc.). The difference is meaning: aby = purpose, kdyby = hypothetical condition. The result clause after kdyby uses 'bych/bys/by' + past participle.

exercises.answer_key: Both use the past participle (-l form)

exercises.prompt: Translate to Czech: 'If I had time, I would go with you.' (male speaker)

data.source: If I had time, I would go with you. (male speaker)
accepted_answers: Kdybych měl čas, šel bych s tebou.
Kdybych měl čas, šel bych s vámi.
Šel bych s tebou, kdybych měl čas.
data.grammar_note: 'Kdybych měl čas, šel bych s tebou.' = If I had time, I would go with you. Two-part structure: (1) kdybych + past participle 'měl' (masc.), (2) past participle 'šel' (masc.) + conditional 'bych'. Note 's tebou' (with you, informal) vs 's vámi' (formal/plural). The kdyby-clause can come first or second.

exercises.answer_key: Kdybych měl čas, šel bych s tebou.

exercises.prompt: Arrange the words: 'If you (sg.) helped me, I would finish it today.'

data.explanation: 'Kdybys mi pomohl, dokončil bych to dnes.' = If you helped me, I would finish it today. Two-part conditional: (1) Kdybys + past participle 'pomohl' (you helped), (2) past participle 'dokončil' + conditional 'bych' (I would finish). Note the subject shift — the helper is 'ty' (you) but the finisher is 'já' (I), so two different conditional forms: kdybys and bych.

exercises.answer_key: Kdybys mi pomohl, dokončil bych to dnes.

exercises.prompt: Complete the sentence by choosing aby or kdyby + the correct form: 'I'm telling you this so that you understand.'

data.explanation: 'Říkám ti to, abys to chápal.' = I'm telling you this so that you understand. 'Abys' = 2nd person singular (you), 'chápal' = past participle of 'chápat' (masc.). This is a purpose clause — we want the person to understand. Compare with kdyby: 'Kdybys to chápal, neptal by ses.' = If you understood it, you wouldn't ask.

exercises.prompt: Pronounce the complex conditional sentence combining purpose and hypothetical condition.

data.target_text: Kdybych měl víc času, učil bych se česky každý den, abych mohl mluvit s tvou rodinou.
data.translation_en: If I had more time, I would study Czech every day so that I could speak with your family.

exercises.answer_key: Kdybych měl víc času, učil bych se česky každý den, abych mohl mluvit s tvou rodinou.
